sections.opening: Sit down, dear one... the candle is lit. We've descended from the Tower, and in pain discovered the truth about our spiritual identity. Now we stand before a new and challenging station in our journey — The Devil. "But Maktuba," you say, "this is a frightening card!" Not in my eyes, dear child. The Devil of the Tarot is not an evil creature from hell. He is our harshest teacher — the one who reveals to us the bondages we ourselves have chosen. In the lesson of The Hierophant we learned about connection to spirituality, here we learn about the connections that bind us. The Devil doesn't come to destroy — he comes to liberate. But he will only do so after we clearly see the cage we've built for ourselves.
sections.theoryA:
What Does The Devil Represent in Tarot and Why Isn't It Frightening?

The Devil in Tarot symbolizes the forces that hold us captive by our own free will. This is the card of emotional bondages, physical dependencies, and destructive patterns that we ourselves have chosen and maintain. In the card's image, the winged figure sits on a throne with people at his feet — bound, but the chains are loose. This is The Devil's great secret: we can always leave, but we've chosen to stay.

Unlike The Tower which represents external destruction, The Devil focuses on our internal choices. This is the card that reveals our comfortable illusions: "I have to do this," "I can't change," "It's stronger than me." The Devil laughs at these statements and says: "Really? See how easy it is to release these chains."

In Jewish mysticism, Satan is the accuser — the one who presents challenges to lead us to spiritual growth. He is not hostile, he is functional. Like a tough fitness trainer who pushes us beyond our perceived limitations. The Devil of the Tarot asks us to recognize our power, take responsibility for our lives, and stop blaming external forces for our condition.

The number 15 (1+5=6) connects The Devil to The Lovers — and this is no coincidence. While The Lovers represent correct choice in love, The Devil represents poor choices in passion and dependency. Both deal with relationships and choices, but The Devil shows us what happens when we choose from fear, dependency or illusion instead of from true love.

practice1.instruction: Take The Devil card and observe it deeply. Identify 3 things in your life that feel like "chains" — things you say "I have to" or "I have no choice." Write them down. Now ask yourself honestly: Is there really no choice, or is it more comfortable to believe there's no choice?

sections.theoryB:
How Does The Devil Connect to Astrology and Kabbalah?

In astrology, The Devil is connected to Capricorn and the planet Saturn. Capricorn represents structures, rules and limitations — exactly what The Devil shows us we've built around ourselves. Saturn, the planet of limitations and hard lessons, teaches us through difficulty and discipline. When we don't learn from Saturn's lessons, we become its slaves — and this is exactly what The Devil reveals.

In Kabbalah, The Devil connects to the Sephirah of Hod — submission and acknowledgment. This is the Sephirah where we learn to humble the ego and recognize our limitations. But when this submission becomes passive surrender and abandonment of personal power, we enter The Devil's energy — self-bondage that disguises itself as humility or fate.

The Devil's Kabbalistic path connects the Sephirah of Hod to Tiphereth (Beauty and Truth). This is the journey from blind submission to discovering the truth about ourselves. The Devil presents us with a test: Do we really need these chains, or can we choose freedom? He forces us to look at destructive patterns and ask — who does this really serve?

Interestingly, in Gematria, the word "Satan" equals the word "Nachash" (serpent) (358). Both represent the force of temptation that leads to knowledge. The serpent in the Garden of Eden wasn't evil — it offered knowledge. Similarly, The Devil of the Tarot offers us knowledge about our patterns, but the price is giving up the innocence and comfort of ignorance.

practice2.instruction: Choose one of the chains you identified in the previous exercise. Write a short letter to yourself from The Devil's perspective, explaining exactly how this chain serves you — what comfort or security it provides. Be honest and sharp. For example: "My dear, your fear of leaving your job serves you because it protects you from being tested."

sections.deepDive:
What's Not Written in the Books — The Devil as Growth Teacher

Most Tarot interpreters present The Devil as a negative card indicating bondage and chains. This is a superficial interpretation that misses the true power of this card. The Devil is actually one of the most important teachers in the Tarot journey — the one who reveals to us that all the "forces" we think are holding us are actually our own choices.

In deep soul work, The Devil represents the critical moment when we discover we are not victims. He shows that every excuse of "I can't" is really "I don't want to" in disguise. This is a painful but liberating discovery — because the moment we see we've chosen, we can also choose differently.

The Devil also reveals the black magic we perform on ourselves — the way we bewitch ourselves into thinking we are powerless. He shows that the most powerful spell is the one we cast on ourselves, and that "curses" are usually mistaken beliefs we choose to hold.

questions.question: What does The Devil primarily symbolize in Tarot?
questions.explanation: The Devil symbolizes bondages and dependencies that we ourselves created and choose to maintain, not external forces

questions.question: Which astrological sign is The Devil connected to?
questions.explanation: The Devil is connected to Capricorn and the planet Saturn, representing structures and limitations

questions.question: What is the Gematric meaning of the word 'Satan'?
questions.explanation: Satan and serpent equal in Gematria (358), and both represent the force of temptation leading to knowledge

questions.question: Which Sephirah in Kabbalah does The Devil connect to?
questions.explanation: The Devil connects to the Sephirah of Hod - submission and acknowledgment, where we learn about limitations and proper surrender

questions.question: What do the chains in The Devil card image show us?
options: That they are strong and unbreakable
That they are loose and we can leave
That they are magical and cannot be removed
That they were imposed on us from outside
questions.explanation: The chains in The Devil card are loose, symbolizing that we can always leave but have chosen to stay

closing.summary: The Devil is not an enemy but a harsh teacher who exposes the bondages we've chosen. He reveals that all our "I have to" are actually disguised "I choose to." Through recognizing our power to choose, we move from blind submission to true freedom.
closing.wisdom: The greatest freedom is to see that you were always free.
closing.nextPreview: In the next lesson we'll meet The Tower — the moment when all false constructions collapse and we discover who we really are beneath the ruins.

faq.question: Is The Devil in Tarot related to the religious Satan?
faq.answer: No. The Devil of the Tarot is a psychological symbol representing internal bondages and dependencies, not a religious entity. This is a card about personal responsibility and free choice.

faq.question: What do you do when The Devil appears in a reading?
faq.answer: Honestly examine where you feel bound or dependent. Ask: What does this bondage give me? How do I choose to maintain it? Where can I choose freedom?

faq.question: How is The Devil different from The Tower?
faq.answer: The Devil focuses on internal choices and bondages we create, while The Tower represents external breakdowns that come from outside. The Devil says 'you chose', The Tower says 'this happens'.

faq.question: Is The Devil always negative?
faq.answer: No. The Devil can be positive when it represents recognition of destructive patterns and freedom from illusions. It's only negative when it represents denial of personal responsibility and remaining in bondage.

seo.title: The Devil in Tarot - Chosen Bondages and True Freedom | Maktuba
seo.description: Discover the true meaning of The Devil card in Tarot - not external evil but internal bondages we've chosen. Learn how to identify and release your emotional chains.
